summary: I was born in the 27th year of the Meiji period, which was 1894. My father often told us about his experiences during those times, especially how Christianity was not freely accepted then. The Meiji period was a time of great change in Japan, and it shaped much of my family's life and faith.

My father was born in Tokyo when it was still called Yedo, and later I came to Tokyo myself for schooling and nursing training. Tokyo was lively even back then, and I remember attending a hospital there where I became a midwife and nurse. It was also in Tokyo that my father had many clients and where our family was connected to the Christian community.

My father was one of the first graduates of what is now Chiba Medical University, back when it was just founded. He chose medicine over the military, despite initially trying to pass the military academy exam. His medical career started in Kemigawa and later Kisarazu, where he worked as a prison and courthouse doctor.

Yamaoka Tesshu was my father's teacher at his private school. My father tried to pass the military academy exam while studying under him but failed the physical due to pleurisy. Yamaoka Tesshu advised my father to keep his faith private at that time, reflecting the challenges Christians faced then.

Our family was affiliated with the Nihon Kirisuto Kyokai, which is a Presbyterian church. We attended services there and had missionaries visit from time to time. This church was a central part of our Christian life in Japan.

My father found 'Pilgrim's Progress' at a bookstore in Nihonbashi and read it before the Bible. It was written in classic Japanese and introduced him to Christian ideas during a time when faith was not freely accepted. This book was important in his spiritual journey.

Along with 'Pilgrim's Progress,' my father also found the Bible in classic Japanese at a bookstore in Nihonbashi. Reading the Bible was a significant part of his faith development, even though Christianity was not yet widely accepted in Japan. This shaped our family's Christian beliefs deeply.

After some years practicing medicine, my father moved to Kisarazu where he worked as a prison doctor and courthouse doctor. Kisarazu was a town that later became a city, and my father was well known there as a medical practitioner. It was part of his career progression after facing difficulties as a Christian doctor.

When I was about ten or eleven years old, the Russo-Japanese War broke out. Russia was Greek Catholic, and because of that, Christians like us were persecuted in Japan during that time. I remember the hostility we faced, such as stones being thrown at me on the way to school and damage to our home during festivals.

During the Russo-Japanese War, I learned that Russia was Greek Catholic, which made people in Japan view Christians like us with suspicion. This connection led to persecution of Christians in our town, as they were seen as foreign and untrustworthy. It was a difficult time for our family and community.

The World's Sunday School Convention was held in Poke around the time I came to America. Mr. Igarashi, who later became my husband, attended as a delegate. This event helped bring us together and was part of the reason I eventually moved to America.

I came to Hawaii because it is a Christian country and shared my family's faith. Although I initially wanted to stay in Tokyo, circumstances and connections, including my marriage to Mr. Igarashi, brought me here. Hawaii became my new home where I continued my Christian life.

Mr. Tasuku Harada was president of Kyoto University before coming to Hawaii to take up the president's post at the University of Hawaii. I was aware of his plans and respected his leadership. His move to Hawaii was part of the broader connection between Japan and Hawaii in education and faith.

The University of Hawaii was where Mr. Tasuku Harada was to become president after leaving Kyoto University. This university was part of the community I joined when I came to Hawaii. It represented a bridge between Japanese and Hawaiian cultures during that time.

Ishii Kikujirō was an ex-Minister of Foreign Affairs and a friend of my cousin. He was close to Mr. Igarashi's parents and was originally supposed to accompany me to America. However, his appointment as ambassador to London delayed my departure, which changed my travel plans.

Tasuku Harada was president of Kyoto University and was coming to Hawaii to become president of the University of Hawaii. I knew of him and his plans, and he was part of the network of Japanese leaders connected to Hawaii. His presence symbolized the educational and cultural ties between Japan and Hawaii.
The transcript does not provide specific information about 'Shunyo Maru,' so I cannot share personal memories or experiences related to it.
There is no direct mention or personal connection to Waseda University in my story, so I cannot provide a summary about it.
My story does not include any experiences or references to the Heart Mountain Relocation Center, so I have no memories to share about it.

As a Japanese Christian woman who came to America, I am connected to the Japanese American community through faith and family. My family maintained Christian beliefs despite hardships in Japan, and coming to America was partly motivated by sharing that faith in a new land. Our experiences reflect the challenges and hopes of Japanese Americans during that era.
